revision.text:
[[ca:Cinc reis de Wa]][[de:Fünf Könige von Wa]][[en:Five kings of Wa]][[es:Cinco reyes de Wa]][[fr:Cinq rois de Wa]][[ja:倭の五王]][[ko:왜5왕]][[pt:Cinco Reis de Wa]][[uk:П'ять королів Ва]][[vi:Oa Ngũ vương]][[zh:倭五王]] The {{Nihongo|'''five kings of [[:en:Wa (Japan)|Wa]]'''|倭の五王|Wa no go ō}} http://www.hongwontack.pe.kr/homepage4/data/450815.pdf {{Bare URL PDF|date=August 2024}} were kings of ancient {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} who sent envoys to {{ill|China|en|China|qid=Q148|lt=China|1=China}} during the 5th century to strengthen the legitimacy of their claims to power by gaining the recognition of a [[:en:Chinese emperor|Chinese emperor]] during the chaotic period of the {{ill|Northern and Southern dynasties|en|Northern and Southern dynasties|qid=Q49696|lt=Northern and Southern dynasties|1=Northern and Southern dynasties}}, when either dynasty was desperate to gain legitimacy over the other by trying to assert itself as the granter of official titles, through garnering as many foreign countries willing to receive titles. Details about the five kings are unknown. According to written records in China, their names were {{ill|San of Wa|en|San of Wa|qid=Q28692297|lt=San|1=San of Wa}} ({{zh|labels=no|t={{linktext|贊}}|l=Laudable}}), {{ill|Chin of Wa|en|Chin of Wa|qid=Q28692298|lt=Chin|1=Chin of Wa}} ({{zh|labels=no|t={{linktext|珍}}|l=Precious}}), [[Sai of Wa|Sai]] ({{zh|labels=no|t={{linktext|濟}}|l=Helpful}}), [[Kō of Wa|Kō]] ({{zh|labels=no|t={{linktext|興}}|l=Prosperous}}) and {{ill|Bu of Wa|en|Bu of Wa|qid=Q28692301|lt=Bu|1=Bu of Wa}} ({{zh|labels=no|t={{linktext|武}}|l=Martial}}). But no kings with such names appear in the Japanese records, and where during this time only 3 appear instead of 5, spurring much debate of their identities. There is also a briefly mentioned king named [[Dei of Wa]] who may have came before the five kings, but whose existence is disputed.{{Cite journal |last=Kishimoto |first=Naofumi |date=2013-05-01 |title=Dual Kingship in the Kofun Period as Seen from the Keyhole Tombs |journal=Journal of Urban Culture Research|s2cid=193255655 }}{{rp||pages=dd}} == Titles for the Kings of Wa == In summary, the monarchs of Wa repeatedly asked for the titles: * {{lang|zh|倭國王}} ''(King of Wa Country)'' * 使持節 ''(Highest Rank Military Commander)'' * {{lang|zh|安東大將軍}} ''(Grand Peacekeeper-General of the East)'' * 都督-region-諸軍事 ''(Commander of All Military Affairs of listed regions)'' The Southern dynasty of [[:en:Liu Song Dynasty|Liu Song]] (not to be confused with the {{ill|Song dynasty|en|Song dynasty|qid=Q7462|lt=Song|1=Song dynasty}}), with the most interaction with the 5 Kings of Wa, readily granted the title of King of Wa on request, but repeatedly refused them the 二品 Class 2, 6th Rank {{lang|zh|安東大將軍}} (Grand Peacekeeper-General of the East), and bestowed them the 三品 Class 3, 3rd Rank {{lang|zh|安東將軍}} (Peacekeeper-General of the East) instead until their final year of reign when their waning power rendered them desperate for allies. === Peacekeeper-General of the East === In the Liu Song, the highest Rank of military commanders were all in Class 2 of the [[Nine-Rank system|Nine-Rank System]] based on the system used in the [[:en:Jin Dynasty (266–420)|Jin Dynasty]] ([[:en:Liang Dynasty|Liang Dynasty]] and {{ill|Chen dynasty|en|Chen dynasty|qid=Q62454|lt=Chen Dynasty|1=Chen dynasty}} used the 武職二十四班 24-Class Martial Occupation system instead). These were also bestowed to the rulers of the subject state (located in the East of China) who sent envoy to the emperor of the Dynasty. The highest general title was {{lang|zh|征東大將軍}} (''Grand General conquering the East''). The next highest title was {{lang|zh|鎮東大將軍}} (''Grand Suppressor-General of the East''). The third highest and the lowest among three was {{lang|zh|安東大將軍}} (''Grand Peacekeeper-General of the East''). In the [[:en:Liu Song Dynasty|Song]] dynasty, the emperors bestowed 3 eastern monarchies (夷): * 413 - {{lang|zh|征東大將軍}} (''Grand Conqueror-General of the East'') to the King of {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|qid=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo|1=Goguryeo}}. Class 2, 4th Rank. ** 463 - promoted 2 Ranks to 車騎大將軍 (''Grand Chariot-General''). Class 2, 2nd Rank. * 416 - {{lang|zh|鎮東將軍}} (''Suppressor-General of the East'') to the King of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]]. Class 3, 2nd Rank. ** 420 - promoted to {{lang|zh|鎮東大將軍}} (''Grand Suppressor-General of the East''). Class 2, 5th Rank. * 421 - {{lang|zh|安東將軍}} (''Peacekeeper-General of the East'') to the King of Wa. Class 3, 3rd Rank. ** 478 - promoted to {{lang|zh|安東大將軍}} (''Grand Peacekeeper-General of the East'') to the King of Wa on the last year of the Liu Song dynasty. Class 2, 6th Rank. Of these 3 eastern countries, only the Wa were recorded to have visited Song already with 自稱 (self-proclaimed) titles. There are interpretations on this fact that the King of Goguryeo stood in the highest Rank, and the King of Wa stood in the lowest Rank, while the King of Baekje stood between Goguryeo and Wa.Ishii 2005, p.169 But there are also other opinions against this interpretation. The difference of general Ranks among the three states (Goguryeo, Baekje, and Wa) was due to the order which each state had first sent envoy to the dynasty, and it cannot be said that the Ranks of Goguryeo and Baekje were substantially higher than that of Wa.Ishii 2005, p.175 Sakamoto also points out the different view on these general titles.(ja) 坂元義種 『倭の五王』教育社、1981st year 9月、180頁。{{ISBN|4315401455}}. But these assertions are refutable; seeing that the Class 2 {{lang|zh|安東大將軍}} title is a specific self-proclaimed title by the Wa, not one where the Wa asked to be have any available Class 2 generalship and the Liu Song deciding to relegate them behind Baekje without Wa's consent. Furthermore, If the Wa in asking for affirmation in their self-proclaimed {{lang|zh|安東大將軍}} title were deliberately humbling themselves to be Ranked behind the Goguryeo and Baekje in the Liu Song hierarchy, then Baekje would not be included in Sai's 438 self-proclaimed Command of All Military Affairs of Six Countries, nor would it be included in Bu's 477 self-proclaimed Command of All Military Affairs of Seven Countries. Wang Jiànqún 王健群, points out that the fact Wa's frequent askance for recognition, undermines credence to their claim, on top of their self-proclaimed titles being rejected and given a lower grade title Song is destroyed in 479, and with a shift in Chinese dynasties, a new titles were issued anew: * '''479''' - Without any envoys sent there, the newfound {{ill|Southern Qi|en|Southern Qi|qid=Q62456|lt=Qi dynasty|1=Southern Qi}} voluntarily gives out titles as a celebratory gesture and establishing itself as a granter of coveted titles. Qi bestows in 479: ** Class 2, 1st Rank {{lang|zh|驃騎大將軍}} (''Grand Fast-Cavalry General'') to the King of {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|qid=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo|1=Goguryeo}}, automatically promoted 1 Rank from Song's 開府儀同三司{{Efn|開府儀同三司 : Person whose Position is equivalent or corresponds to the "Three Lords" (Highest Ranking Officials in the old Imperial Chinese governments. That is, 丞相 (司徒), 太尉, 御史大夫 (司空).}} and Class 2, 2nd Rank 車騎大將軍 (''Grand Chariot-General''), titles for the King of Goguryeo.{{Cite web |title=南齊書 : 卷五十八列傳第三十九 蠻 東南夷 - 中國哲學書電子化計劃 |url=https://ctext.org/wiki.pl?if=gb&chapter=415718 |access-date=2023-08-27 |website=ctext.org |language=zh-TW}} ** Class 2, 5th Rank {{lang|zh|鎮東大將軍}} (''Grand Suppressor-General of the East'') to the King of Wa, from Class 2, 6th Rank {{lang|zh|安東大將軍}}. *** The King of Baekje would receive the same {{lang|zh|鎮東大將軍}} title upon sending an envoy in 495. ** Class 3, 14th Rank {{lang|zh|輔國將軍}} (''Assisting'' ''General'') to the [[Haji of Gaya]], but not automatically, it sent an envoy in 479. * '''502''' - Without any envoys sent there, the newfound [[:en:Liang Dynasty|Liang Dynasty]] voluntarily gives out titles as a celebratory gesture and establishing itself as a granter of coveted titles. The Liang first used 24-Class (班) system where the higher number denoted higher Rank, until later adding up to 34 classes. Liang bestows in 502: ** 車騎大將軍 (''Grand Chariot-General'') to {{ill|Munjamyeong of Goguryeo|en|Munjamyeong of Goguryeo|qid=Q498827|lt=Munjamyeong of Goguryeo|1=Munjamyeong of Goguryeo}}, from 車騎將軍 (''Chariot-General''). Promotion from 24th Class, 6th Rank to 3rd Rank. ** 征東大將軍 (''Grand Conqueror-General of the East'') to {{ill|Dongseong of Baekje|en|Dongseong of Baekje|qid=Q497846|lt=Dongseong of Baekje|1=Dongseong of Baekje}}, from {{lang|zh|鎮東大將軍}} (''Grand Suppressor-General of the East''). Promotion from 23rd Class, 3rd Rank to 24th Class, 7th Rank. ** 征東大將軍 (''Grand Conqueror-General of the East'') to Bu of Wa, from {{lang|zh|鎮東大將軍}} (''Grand Suppressor-General of the East''). Promotion from 23rd Class, 3rd Rank to 24th Class, 7th Rank. ** 鎮西將軍 (''Suppressor-General of the West'') to King Liangmi He of [[:en:Dangchang|DangChang]], from 安西將軍 (''Peacekeeper-General of the West'') Promotion from 21st Class, 3rd Rank to 22nd Class, 3rd Rank. ** 征西將軍 (''Conqueror-General of the West'') to King Xiuliudai of {{ill|Tuyuhun|en|Tuyuhun|qid=Q1196201|lt=Tuyuhun|1=Tuyuhun}} from 鎮西將軍 (''Suppressor-General of the West''). Promotion from 23rd Class, 3rd Rank to 22nd Class, 3rd Rank. === Regional Appointments === Comparing contemporary historical records of the regions in the Korean peninsula around this time, and numerous discrepancies listed below, it is obvious these claims are false. In context, exploiting China's lack of interest in eastern matters to lay false claim over neighboring territories was not a new custom in East Asia. Baekje itself {{ill|Portraits of Periodical Offering|en|Portraits of Periodical Offering|qid=Q3278527|lt=had claimed that Silla is under its control prior|1=Portraits of Periodical Offering}}; Goguryeo claimed Baekje and Silla are both under its control. * '''438''' - Chin petitions official appointment of the self-proclaimed title, Commander of All Military Affairs of: 倭百濟新羅任那秦韓慕韓六國 ** 6 Countries of Wa - Baekje - Silla - Imna - Jinhan - Mohan (Mahan) ** Of note are Jinhan and Mohan, countries that have already ceased to exist centuries ago. This already undermines his claim in both that they do not exist, or would be redundant locales as they are now part of other countries: *** Countries of the {{ill|Jinhan confederacy|en|Jinhan confederacy|qid=Q701273|lt=Jinhan Confederacy|1=Jinhan confederacy}} were one by one annexed into Silla around 1st to 3rd century. *** Countries of the {{ill|Mahan confederacy|en|Mahan confederacy|qid=Q704278|lt=Mohan (Mahan) Confederacy|1=Mahan confederacy}} were one by one annexed into Baekje by the 4th century. * '''451''' - Liu Song entitles Sai to Commander of All Military Affairs of: 倭新羅任那加羅秦韓慕韓六國 ** 6 Countries of Wa - Silla - Imna - Gaya - Jinhan - Mohan (Mahan) ** Liu Song did not approve of the Wa laying claim to Baekje and removed it from their entitlement.坂元義種「巻頭史論 研究史 古代の日本と朝鮮--「七支刀」「好太王碑」「倭の五王」研究の成果と課題」『歴史読本』第51巻第3号、新人物往来社、2006th year 2月、 58-59頁 This is because {{ill|Jeonji of Baekje|en|Jeonji of Baekje|qid=Q497705|lt=Jeonji of Baekje|1=Jeonji of Baekje}} already contacted Liu Song and was recognized as {{lang|zh|鎮東將軍}} (''Suppressor-General of the East'') in 416, and {{lang|zh|鎮東大將軍}} (''Grand Suppressor-General of the East'') in 420. The other countries were not in contact with Song. ** Instead, without Sai requesting it, Liu Song appointed Sai to the [[:en:Gaya confederacy|Gaya Confederacy]], which was interchangeable with the region of Imna. This sloppy redundancy is an important evidence that shows Liu Song's own half-hearted attitude. * '''477''' - Bu petitions official appointment of self-proclaimed title, Commander of All Military Affairs of: 倭百濟新羅任那加羅秦韓慕韓七國 ** 7 Countries of Wa - Baekje - Silla - Imna - Gaya - Jinhan - Mohan (Mahan) ** Of note is Bu going along with Liu Song's sloppy mistake of adding in Gaya, showing he too knows as little as the Liu Song of the lands he claims to rule over, undermining the claim's credence even further. ** Bu requests reaffirmation of being appointed to what Sai was appointed to, in addition to reattempting to be appointed to Baekje. * '''478''' - Liu Song entitles Bu to Commander of All Military Affairs of: 倭新羅任那加羅秦韓慕韓六國 ** 6 Countries of Wa - Silla - Imna - Gaya - Jinhan - Mohan (Mahan) ** Liu Song once again ignores Wa's request to add in Baekje, and only reaffirms what Sai was appointed to in 451. Also, a few entries prior to the Kings of Wa, the Book of Song in introducing the Baekje, described that Baekje rules the {{ill|Liaoxi|en|Liaoxi|qid=Q1054752|lt=Liaoxi|1=Liaoxi}} region while Goguryeo rules the {{ill|en:Liaodong Peninsula|en|en:Liaodong Peninsula|qid=Q326917|lt=Liaodong|1=Liaodong Peninsula}} region.{{Cite web |title=宋書 : 卷九十七列傳第五十七 夷蠻 - 中國哲學書電子化計劃 |url=https://ctext.org/wiki.pl?if=gb&chapter=474169 |access-date=2023-08-27 |website=ctext.org |language=zh-TW}} This blatant inaccuracy is also evidence that Liu Song was rather uninformed or uninterested in keeping accurate knowledge of geopolitical situations out east. == Chinese records == {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! {{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|qid=Q12138|lt=Gregorian date|1=Gregorian calendar}} (CE) ! [[:en:Dynasties in Chinese history|Dynasty]] ! {{ill|Chinese calendar|en|Chinese calendar|qid=Q134032|lt=Chinese calendar|1=Chinese calendar}} ! {{ill|Twenty-Four Histories|en|Twenty-Four Histories|qid=Q175077|lt=Original Chinese source|1=Twenty-Four Histories}} ! King of Wa ! class="unsortable" width="50%" | Short summary{{efn|These titles for sovereignty over the states described had no bearing on reality. The appointments reflected the struggle for hegemony over the Korean Peninsula between {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|qid=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo|1=Goguryeo}} and Wa, as depicted in the {{ill|Gwanggaeto Stele|en|Gwanggaeto Stele|qid=Q701096|lt=Gwanggaeto Stele|1=Gwanggaeto Stele}}.吉田晶, ''倭王権の時代'', 新日本出版社, 1998}} |- | align="center" | {{nts|413}} | {{center|[[:en:Jin Dynasty (266–420)|Jin]]}} |9th year of Yìxī (義熙九年) | {{center|{{ill|Book of Jin|en|Book of Jin|qid=Q1154753|lt=Book of Jin|1=Book of Jin}}, {{ill|Taiping Yulan|en|Taiping Yulan|qid=Q586049|lt=Taiping Yulan|1=Taiping Yulan}}}} | {{center|Unknown{{efn|This is listed under "King San" in the {{ill|Book of Liang|en|Book of Liang|qid=Q1285767|lt=Book of Liang|1=Book of Liang}}}}}} | The king of Wa sent tribute. |- | align="center" | {{nts|421}} | {{center|[[:en:Liu Song Dynasty|Song]]}} |2nd year of Yǒngchū (永初二年) | {{center|{{ill|Book of Song|en|Book of Song|qid=Q1143142|lt=Book of Song|1=Book of Song}}}} | {{center|{{ill|San of Wa|en|San of Wa|qid=Q28692297|lt=San|1=San of Wa}}}} | King San sent tribute to Jin. [[:en:Emperor Wu of Liu Song|Emperor Wu of Song]] bestowed the title, {{lang|zh|安東將軍}} (''Peacekeeper-General of the East'') and {{lang|zh|倭國王}} (''King of Wa''), on San. |- | align="center" | {{nts|425}} | {{center|Song}} | 2nd year of Yuánjiā (元嘉二年) | {{center|Book of Song}} | {{center|San}} | King San sent Shiba Sōtatsu ({{lang|ja|司馬曹達}}){{efn|In this case, ''Shiba'' might be the title of Sōtatsu, rather than his surname.(ja) [https://japanknowledge.com/introduction/keyword.html?i=2078 Nipponica] {{lang|ja|日本大百科全書（ニッポニカ）, 倭の五王}} 坂元義種 (Retrieved 2023-0209) Sōtatsu might be a member of ''{{ill|Toraijin|en|Toraijin|qid=Q11561926|lt=Toraijin|1=Toraijin}}'' group.}} as an envoy and made [[:en:Emperor Wen of Liu Song|Emperor Wen of Song]] a present. |- | align="center" | {{nts|430}} | {{center|Song}} | 7th year of Yuánjiā (元嘉七年) | {{center|Book of Song}} | {{center|Unknown{{efn|Possibly King San}}}} | In January, the king of Wa sent tribute. |- | align="center" | {{nts|438}} | {{center|Song}} | 15th year of Yuánjiā (元嘉十五年) | {{center|Book of Song}} | {{center|{{ill|Chin of Wa|en|Chin of Wa|qid=Q28692298|lt=Chin|1=Chin of Wa}}}} | King San died, his younger brother Chin succeeded the throne. Chin sent tribute and self-proclaimed the title of {{lang|zh|使持節都督倭百濟新羅任那秦韓慕韓六國諸軍事安東大將軍倭國王}}.
In April, Emperor Wen appointed Chin to the title of {{lang|zh|安東將軍}} (''Peacekeeper-General of the East'') and {{lang|zh|倭國王}} (''King of Wa'').
The emperor also appointed Wa Zui ({{lang|ja|倭隋}}) and other 13 subordinates of Chin to the titles of {{lang|zh|平西征虜冠軍輔國將軍}}. |- | align="center" | {{nts|443}} | {{center|Song}} | 20th year of Yuánjiā (元嘉二十年) | {{center|Book of Song}} | {{center|[[Sai of Wa|Sai]]}} | Sai sent tribute and was appointed to the title of and {{lang|zh|安東將軍}} (''Peacekeeper-General of the East'') and {{lang|zh|倭國王}} (''King of Wa''). |- | align="center" | {{nts|451}} | {{center|Song}} | 28th year of Yuánjiā (元嘉二十八年) | {{center|Book of Song}} | {{center|Sai}} | In July, King Sai was appointed to the title of {{lang|zh|使持節都督倭新羅任那加羅秦韓慕韓六國諸軍事}} and {{lang|zh|安東將軍}} (''Peacekeeper-General of the East'') and {{lang|zh|倭國王}} (''King of Wa''). 23 subordinates were also promoted. |- | align="center" | {{nts|460}} | {{center|Song}} | 4th year of Dàmíng (大明四年) | | {{center|Unknown{{efn|Possibly King Sai}}}} | In December, the king of Wa sent a tribute. |- | align="center" | {{nts|462}} | {{center|Song}} | 6th year of Dàmíng (大明六年) | {{center|Book of Song}} | {{center|[[Kō of Wa|Kō]]}} | In March, [[:en:Emperor Xiaowu of Liu Song|Emperor Xiaowu of Song]] appointed Kō, a crown prince of Sai, to the title of {{lang|zh|安東將軍}} (''Peacekeeper-General of the East'') and {{lang|zh|倭國王}} (''King of Wa''). |- | align="center" | {{nts|477}} | {{center|Song}} |1st year of Shēngmíng (昇明元年) | {{center|Book of Song}} | {{center|{{ill|Bu of Wa|en|Bu of Wa|qid=Q28692301|lt=Bu|1=Bu of Wa}}}} | In November, the king of Wa sent a tribute.
King Kō died, his younger brother Bu succeeded to the throne. Bu self-proclaimed the title of {{lang|zh|使持節都督倭百濟新羅任那加羅秦韓慕韓七國諸軍事}} and {{lang|zh|安東大將軍}} (''Grand'' ''Peacekeeper-General of the East'') and {{lang|zh|倭國王}} (''King of Wa''). |- | align="center" | {{nts|478}} | {{center|Song}} | 2nd year of Shēngmíng (昇明二年) | {{center|Book of Song}} | {{center|Bu}} | Bu self-proclaimed the title of 開府儀同三司 and petitioned the official appointment. [[:en:Emperor Shun of Liu Song|Emperor Shun of Song]] appointed Bu to the title of {{lang|zh|使持節都督倭新羅任那加羅秦韓慕韓六國諸軍事}} and {{lang|zh|安東大將軍}} (''Grand'' ''Peacekeeper-General of the East'') and {{lang|zh|倭王}}. |- | align="center" | {{nts|479}} | {{center|{{ill|Southern Qi|en|Southern Qi|qid=Q62456|lt=Qi|1=Southern Qi}}}} |1st year of Jiànyuán (建元元年) | {{center|{{ill|Book of Qi|en|Book of Qi|qid=Q1192857|lt=Book of Qi|1=Book of Qi}}}} | {{center|Bu}} | Without being requested, {{ill|Emperor Gao of Southern Qi|en|Emperor Gao of Southern Qi|qid=Q1194981|lt=Emperor Gao|1=Emperor Gao of Southern Qi}} of the newly founded of {{ill|Southern Qi|en|Southern Qi|qid=Q62456|lt=Qi dynasty|1=Southern Qi}} promoted numerous foreign monarchs as a celebratory gesture, such as Bu to the title of {{lang|zh|鎮東大將軍}} (''Grand Suppressor-General of the East''.{{efn|name="promotion"|The promotions in 479 and 502 were automatic given the establishment of new dynasties in China.}} |- | align="center" | {{nts|502}} | {{center|[[:en:Liang Dynasty|Liang]]}} |1st year of Tiānjiān (天監元年) | {{center|{{ill|Book of Liang|en|Book of Liang|qid=Q1285767|lt=Book of Liang|1=Book of Liang}}}} | {{center|Bu (Deceased)}} | Without being requested, {{ill|Emperor Wu of Liang|en|Emperor Wu of Liang|qid=Q736726|lt=Emperor Wu|1=Emperor Wu of Liang}} of the newly founded of [[:en:Liang Dynasty|Liang Dynasty]] promoted numerous foreign monarchs as a celebratory gesture, such as Bu, to the title of {{lang|zh|征東大將軍}} (''Grand General conquering the East'').{{efn|name="promotion"}} Bu was already deceased, however. |} ==Comparison with the Nihon Shoki== {{More citations needed|date=June 2007}}{{Five kings of Wa}} As the name of kings recorded in [[:en:Chinese history|Chinese history]] are very different from the names of Emperors in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', the specification of which emperor was the one recorded is the subject of numerous disputes which have endured for centuries. Most contemporary historians assign the five Japanese kings to the following emperors (two possibilities are identified for the Kings San and Chin), mostly based on the individual features of their genealogies reported in the Chinese sources. On the other hand, archeological evidence, such as the inscriptions on the {{ill|Inariyama Sword|en|Inariyama Sword|qid=Q856395|lt=Inariyama|1=Inariyama Sword}} and {{ill|Eta Funayama Sword|en|Eta Funayama Sword|qid=Q3589377|lt=Eta Funayama Sword|1=Eta Funayama Sword}}, also supports the idea that Bu is an equivalent of Emperor Yūryaku, who was called ''Wakatakeru Ōkimi'' by his contemporaries. *{{ill|San of Wa|en|San of Wa|qid=Q28692297|lt=San|1=San of Wa}} {{lang|ja|讃}}: {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|en|Emperor Nintoku|qid=Q313119|lt=Emperor Nintoku|1=Emperor Nintoku}} or {{ill|Emperor Richū|en|Emperor Richū|qid=Q329704|lt=Emperor Richū|1=Emperor Richū}} *{{ill|Chin of Wa|en|Chin of Wa|qid=Q28692298|lt=Chin|1=Chin of Wa}} {{lang|ja|珍}}: {{ill|Emperor Hanzei|en|Emperor Hanzei|qid=Q329769|lt=Emperor Hanzei|1=Emperor Hanzei}} or Emperor Nintoku *[[Sai of Wa|Sai]] {{lang|ja|濟}}: {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|en|Emperor Ingyō|qid=Q349258|lt=Emperor Ingyō|1=Emperor Ingyō}} *[[Kō of Wa|Kō]] {{lang|ja|興}}: {{ill|Emperor Ankō|en|Emperor Ankō|qid=Q361010|lt=Emperor Ankō|1=Emperor Ankō}} *{{ill|Bu of Wa|en|Bu of Wa|qid=Q28692301|lt=Bu|1=Bu of Wa}} {{lang|ja|武}}: {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|qid=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|1=Emperor Yūryaku}} Since Bu is most likely to be Yūryaku, Kō, who is said to be Bu's older brother, is likely to be an equivalent of Ankō, who is also noted in the ''Nihonshoki'' as an elder brother to Yūryaku. However, the ''{{ill|Book of Song|en|Book of Song|qid=Q1143142|lt=Book of Song|1=Book of Song}}'' records Kō as "''Crown Prince'' Kō"; there is a possibility that he is not Ankō, but rather {{ill|Prince Kinashi no Karu|en|Prince Kinashi no Karu|qid=Q7244124|lt=Prince Kinashi no Karu|1=Prince Kinashi no Karu}}, who was a crown prince of Ingyō. ==See also== {{Portal|Ancient Japan}} *[[:en:Kofun Period|Kofun Period]] * {{ill|Wakoku|en|Wakoku|qid=Q10888424|lt=Wakoku|1=Wakoku}} ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== * 石井正敏 (2005th year 6月). (ja) [https://web.archive.org/web/20151018092747/http://www.jkcf.or.jp/history_arch/first/1/1-03-ishii_j.pdf “5世紀の日韓関係 - 倭の五王と高句麗・百済 -”]. ''日韓歴史共同研究報告書（第1期）'' (日韓歴史共同研究). [http://www.jkcf.or.jp/history_arch/first/1/1-03-ishii_j.pdf オリジナル] の2015th year 10月18日時点におけるアーカイブ。 {{Yamatai}} [[en:Five kings of Wa]] [[ca:Cinc reis de Wa]] [[de:Fünf Könige von Wa]] [[es:Cinco reyes de Wa]] [[fr:Cinq rois de Wa]] [[ja:倭の五王]] [[ko:왜5왕]] [[pt:Cinco Reis de Wa]] [[uk:П'ять королів Ва]] [[vi:Oa Ngũ vương]] [[zh:倭五王]] {{wikidata link|Q1152880}} [[Category:Five kings of Wa]] [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Yamatai]] [[Category:Yamato Kingship]] [[Category:Pages with 50+ untranslated japanese characters]] [[Category:Pages with 100+ untranslated japanese characters]] [[Category:Pages with 150+ untranslated japanese characters]]
